The Episcopal Inquisition
nytimes_articles.abstract:
It is a sad paradox that Christianity, which began as a heretical Jewish sect and suffered centuries of persecution, quickly became so strikingly repressive of deviants. As soon as their faith was legalized in the fourth century, Christians began imposing uniformity of belief. Heretics were subject to exile, confiscations of property, branding and death. The very words "heresy trial" evoke the Spanish Inquisition and other shameful episodes of Christian history. 

     Walter C. Righter, a retired Episcopal bishop, is unlikely to suffer torture  at his heresy trial, which begins today in Wilmington, Del. He merely faces suspension from clerical duties for teaching "doctrine contrary to that held by  the Church" -- he signed a document supporting the ordination of noncelibate homosexuals and ordained a gay man as a deacon. Yet the fact that 25 percent of  the Episcopal bishops in the United States have demanded that he be put on trial shows a disturbing trend both in the church and in American society.

Who Says Republicans Are Free Traders?
nytimes_articles.abstract:
In the New Hampshire primary, Patrick Buchanan pressed the hot button that energized many 19th-century Presidential campaigns. Blasting one-sided trade deals that he says cost American workers jobs, he reinvigorated the economic nationalist tradition in the Republican Party. 

     Many Republicans think Mr. Buchanan's views are un-Republican. Since World War II, the party establishment has embraced free trade and economic internationalism as a grand panacea for global problems. However, from a historical perspective, Mr. Buchanan best exemplifies traditional Republican thinking on trade issues.

Nuclear Middlemen
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The Senate is scheduled to hold hearings today on a dangerous new treaty negotiated by the Clinton Administration that would lift longstanding controls on nuclear trade. Unless the Senate rejects the treaty, it will go into effect within two months. 

     The agreement grants the 15 members of Euratom -- the European Union's atomic energy agency -- the right to sell without our approval plutonium that they have extracted from spent United States nuclear fuel. Under the previous accord, which lapsed in December, we had veto power over all European exports of nuclear materials of American origin. These are not insignificant quantities. Three-quarters of the plutonium being produced by Euratom nations today is derived from United States-supplied nuclear fuel.

Justice Denied in Oklahoma
nytimes_articles.abstract:
In explaining his decision to move the Oklahoma City bombing trial to Denver, Federal Judge Richard P. Matsch said that it would not be possible to impanel an impartial jury in Oklahoma because the extensive local media coverage had "demonized" the defendants, Timothy J. McVeigh and Terry L. Nichols. 

     Judge Matsch's worries over the the location of the trial are understandable. But in showing such concern over venue, he apparently gave little consideration  to the question of who should serve as jurors, the legal principle that lawyers  call "vicinage" (pronounced VISS-i-nij). The people of the Western District of Oklahoma need and deserve the chance to pass judgment on the defendants. By refusing to allow them to do so, Judge Matsch deprived them of their right to show they are capable of self-governance.

wiki_events.headline: Faucett Perú Flight 251 crashes in the Andes; all 123 passengers and crew are killed.

pages.title: Faucett Perú Flight 251
pages.abstract: Faucett Perú Flight 251 was a scheduled domestic flight from Lima to Tacna, with a stopover in Arequipa. On 29 February 1996, while completing the first leg, the Boeing 737-200 operating the route crashed on approach to Rodríguez Ballón International Airport. All 123 passengers and crew aboard the aircraft lost their lives in the accident. It is the deadliest aviation accident to occur on Peruvian soil.

pages.title: Andes
pages.abstract: The Andes, Andes Mountains or Andean Mountain Range are the longest continental mountain range in the world, forming a continuous highland along the western edge of South America. The range is 8,900 km (5,500 mi) long and 200 to 700 km wide and has an average height of about 4,000 m (13,000 ft). The Andes extend from south to north through seven South American countries: Argentina, Chile, Bolivia, Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, and Venezuela.

wiki_events.headline: The Siege of Sarajevo officially ends.

pages.title: Siege of Sarajevo
pages.abstract: The siege of Sarajevo was a prolonged military blockade of Sarajevo, the capital of the Republic of Bosnia and Herzegovina, during the ethnically charged Bosnian War. After it was initially besieged by Serbian forces of the Yugoslav People's Army, the city was then besieged by the Army of Republika Srpska. Lasting from 5 April 1992 to 29 February 1996, it was three times longer than the Battle of Stalingrad and more than a year longer than the siege of Leningrad, making it the longest siege of a capital city in the history of modern warfare.
